periods.name: Colonial Brazil
periods.description: After Portugal claimed the territory in 1500, the region gradually became an important part of the Portuguese Empire. Over time, permanent settlements, plantation agriculture, slavery, and territorial expansion transformed the colony into Portugal's most valuable possession in the Americas.

subperiods.name: Pre-Colonial Period
subperiods.description: Portugal claimed the territory but focused mainly on extracting resources and trading with Indigenous peoples rather than building a large colonial administration.

events.description: Pedro Álvares Cabral reached the Brazilian coast and claimed the territory for Portugal.
Portugal launched its first major colonization effort after realizing that simply claiming the territory was not enough to secure it.

subperiods.name: Early Colonization
subperiods.description: Portugal began settling the territory in order to defend it from foreign rivals and turn it into a profitable colony.

events.description: The territory was divided into hereditary captaincies that were granted to private administrators.
Most captaincies struggled, revealing the need for stronger centralized control.

subperiods.name: Governorate and Territorial Expansion
subperiods.description: After the captaincy system proved insufficient, Portugal centralized colonial administration. During this period Brazil expanded far beyond its original borders and became one of the most important parts of the Portuguese Empire.

events.description: The Governorate General was established to strengthen royal authority in the colony.
Salvador was founded as the colonial capital.
The Iberian Union united Portugal and Spain under the same monarch.
Portuguese settlers increasingly expanded beyond the limits established by the Treaty of Tordesillas.
Portugal regained its independence from Spain, ending the Iberian Union.
Dutch forces were expelled from northeastern Brazil after decades of conflict.
The Treaty of Madrid recognized much of the territory occupied beyond the old Tordesillas line.
The colonial capital was moved from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro as the economic center shifted southward.
The Inconfidência Mineira revealed growing dissatisfaction with Portuguese rule.
The Bahia Conspiracy reflected the spread of revolutionary ideas inspired by events in Europe and the Americas.
The Portuguese royal court moved to Rio de Janeiro after Napoleon invaded Portugal.
Brazil ceased to function as a typical colony because the imperial government was now operating from its territory.

periods.name: United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves
periods.description: With the Portuguese court already established in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil was elevated from colony to a constituent kingdom. This change increased local political ambitions and made a return to colonial status increasingly unacceptable to Brazilian elites.

leaders.name: John VI
leaders.title: King of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves

events.description: Brazil was elevated to the status of a kingdom within the United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves.
The Liberal Revolution in Portugal demanded the king's return and sought to restore Brazil's subordinate status.
John VI returned to Portugal but left his son Pedro in Brazil as regent.
Political pressure from Portugal convinced many Brazilian elites that independence was preferable to recolonization.

periods.name: Empire of Brazil
periods.description: Brazil became independent after tensions between local elites and the Portuguese government made continued union increasingly difficult. Unlike most of Spanish America, independence produced a unified monarchy rather than several separate states.

subperiods.name: First Reign
subperiods.description: The new country needed to secure its independence and build national institutions while balancing the interests of different political groups.

events.description: Brazil declared independence from Portugal.
Brazil adopted its first constitution.
The Confederation of the Equator rebellion challenged the authority of the new imperial government.
Brazil recognized the independence of Uruguay after conflict in the Cisplatine region.
Pedro I abdicated the throne and returned to Europe.
The emperor's abdication left the country under the rule of regents until Pedro II reached adulthood.

subperiods.name: Regency Period
subperiods.description: A period of political instability and intense debate between liberals and conservatives, characterized by various regional revolts as the country adjusted to the absence of a monarch.

events.description: The Ragamuffin War (Guerra dos Farrapos) broke out in the south, marking the longest regional revolt.
The Cabanagem revolt began in the Grão-Pará province.
The 'Majority Declaration' (Golpe da Maioridade) was enacted, prematurely declaring Pedro II of age to assume the throne.

subperiods.name: Second Reign
subperiods.description: A long period of relative stability, parliamentary evolution, and economic growth based on coffee production.

events.description: The Eusébio de Queirós Law was passed, effectively ending the transatlantic slave trade.
The Paraguayan War began, the largest international conflict in South American history.
The Paraguayan War concluded, contributing to the internal crisis of the monarchy.
The Golden Law (Lei Áurea) was signed, abolishing slavery in Brazil.
A military coup overthrew the monarchy and established the Republic of Brazil.

periods.name: First Brazilian Republic
periods.description: Also known as the 'Old Republic', this period was dominated by the political influence of coffee-producing elites, a system often referred to as 'coffee with milk' politics.

subperiods.name: Sword Republic (República da Espada)
subperiods.description: The initial period of the republic, marked by military rule and institutional instability while drafting the new constitution.

events.description: Proclamation of the Republic, ending the monarchy.
Promulgation of the first Republican Constitution of Brazil.
Federalist Revolution broke out in the South.

subperiods.name: Oligarchic Republic
subperiods.description: Period characterized by the dominance of powerful state elites, particularly from São Paulo and Minas Gerais.

events.description: End of the War of Canudos.
Implementation of the 'Funding Loan' to renegotiate external debts.
Vaccine Revolt (Revolta da Vacina) in Rio de Janeiro.
Taubaté Agreement to regulate coffee prices.
Revolt of the Lash (Revolta da Chibata).
Brazil declares war on the Central Powers in World War I.
Modern Art Week in São Paulo.
Tenentista revolt in São Paulo.
The Revolution of 1930 overthrew the government, ending the Old Republic.

periods.name: Vargas Era
periods.description: A period defined by the leadership of Getúlio Vargas, shifting from revolutionary government to dictatorship (Estado Novo) and focusing on industrialization and social legislation.

events.description: Constitutionalist Revolution in São Paulo.
Implementation of the Estado Novo dictatorship.
Creation of the CLT (Consolidação das Leis do Trabalho).
End of the Vargas dictatorship and return to democracy.

periods.name: Second Brazilian Republic
periods.description: A period of democratic consolidation and rapid industrialization, marked by political polarization between nationalist and liberal forces.

subperiods.name: Democratic Transition
subperiods.description: After the fall of the Estado Novo, Brazil held free elections and drafted a new democratic constitution, entering a period of competitive multiparty politics.

events.description: A new democratic constitution was promulgated, establishing direct elections and civil liberties.
The Brazilian Communist Party was banned amid Cold War pressures.

subperiods.name: Populist Democracy
subperiods.description: A turbulent period defined by nationalism, developmentalism, and growing social tensions.

events.description: Creation of Petrobras, the state oil company.
Vargas took his own life amid a severe political crisis and military pressure for his resignation.
Launch of the 'Fifty Years in Five' development program, aiming to accelerate industrialization.
Inauguration of Brasília as the new federal capital.
Quadros resigned after only seven months in office, triggering a constitutional crisis.
A parliamentary system was temporarily adopted as a compromise to allow Goulart to take office.
A referendum restored full presidential powers to Goulart.
A military coup ousted Goulart, ending the democratic republic and beginning two decades of military rule.

periods.name: Military Dictatorship
periods.description: The armed forces governed Brazil for over two decades, suppressing political opposition, censoring the press, and overseeing both an

subperiods.name: Hard-line Military Rule
subperiods.description: The most repressive phase of the dictatorship, particularly after 1968, when Institutional Act No. 5 suspended political rights, closed Congress, and gave the government sweeping powers to detain and prosecute opponents.

events.description: Institutional Act No. 1 stripped political rights from opponents and purged the civil service and military.
A new authoritarian constitution was enacted, concentrating power in the executive branch.
Institutional Act No. 5 (AI-5) was issued, marking the beginning of the most brutal phase of repression.
Student protests and labor strikes were violently suppressed.
Brazil won the FIFA World Cup in Mexico, which the regime used for nationalist propaganda.
The economic miracle peaked, with GDP growth exceeding 10% annually, though income inequality deepened.
Construction of the Transamazon Highway began as part of large-scale Amazon development projects.

subperiods.name: Gradual Opening (Abertura)
subperiods.description: A controlled opening process initiated by the military itself, gradually restoring civil liberties, allowing opposition parties to organize, and paving the way for a civilian transition.

events.description: Geisel announced a slow, gradual, and secure (lenta, gradual e segura) political opening.
The April Package imposed new political restrictions to offset opposition gains in congressional elections.
AI-5 was revoked and a political amnesty law was enacted for both military and opposers.
A political party reform ended the two-party system and allowed the reorganization of civilian parties.
The Diretas Já movement mobilized millions of Brazilians demanding direct presidential elections.
The constitutional amendment for direct elections was defeated in Congress by a narrow margin.
Tancredo Neves was elected president by the Electoral College, marking the end of military rule, though he died before taking office.

periods.name: New Republic (Nova República)
periods.description: Brazil's ongoing democratic period, initiated after the end of military rule. The era was defined by the drafting of a new constitution, economic stabilization, institutional consolidation, and an expansion of social rights.

subperiods.name: Democratic Transition and Constitutional Assembly
subperiods.description: The first years of civilian rule were marked by economic instability, high inflation, and the drafting of a new democratic constitution that sought to permanently dismantle the authoritarian legacy.

events.description: Sarney assumed the presidency after the death of president-elect Tancredo Neves.
The Cruzado Plan attempted to control hyperinflation through a price freeze, but ultimately failed.
The Citizen Constitution was promulgated, enshrining broad social and political rights.
Brazil held its first direct presidential election in 29 years.

subperiods.name: Economic Reform and Instability
subperiods.description: Brazil attempted economic liberalization and struggled with chronic inflation. The period was overshadowed by a major presidential corruption scandal that led to the first impeachment in the country's history.

events.description: The Collor Plan froze private bank accounts in an attempt to curb hyperinflation.
Collor resigned amid a corruption scandal and was subsequently impeached by the Senate.
The Real Plan was launched, introducing the new currency and successfully taming hyperinflation.

subperiods.name: Stabilization and Reform
subperiods.description: A period of macroeconomic stabilization under the Real Plan, market-oriented reforms, and gradual social improvements, though structural inequality and unemployment remained persistent challenges.

events.description: Privatization of state companies accelerated as part of a broader liberalization agenda.
FHC was re-elected under a constitutional amendment allowing presidential reelection.
A currency crisis forced Brazil to float the real and seek IMF assistance.
An energy rationing crisis caused by drought exposed structural vulnerabilities in the power sector.

subperiods.name: Social Development and Commodity Boom
subperiods.description: An era of significant poverty reduction and social inclusion, driven by redistributive policies such as Bolsa Família and fueled by a global commodity boom.

events.description: The Bolsa Família conditional cash transfer program was created, becoming one of the largest anti-poverty initiatives in the world.
Lula was reelected with a broad popular mandate.
Large pre-salt oil reserves were discovered off the coast, transforming Brazil's energy prospects.
Brazil achieved 7.5% GDP growth, one of its highest rates in decades.
Mass protests swept the country, beginning over bus fare increases but broadening into wider discontent with public services and corruption.
The Lava Jato (Car Wash) corruption investigation began, exposing a vast bribery scheme involving Petrobras and major contractors.
Rousseff was impeached and removed from office by the Senate on charges related to fiscal accounting practices.

subperiods.name: Political and Institutional Crisis
subperiods.description: A period of deep political polarization, economic hardship, and institutional strain. The COVID-19 pandemic further destabilized the country, and the period ended with a transition back to the left after a closely contested and turbulent election.

events.description: A labor reform and a pension reform proposal were passed, reducing workers' protections and raising the retirement age.
Temer himself was implicated in the Lava Jato investigation but was protected from prosecution by Congress.
Deforestation in the Amazon accelerated markedly amid the rollback of environmental protections.
Brazil became one of the countries hardest hit by the COVID-19 pandemic, recording over 700,000 deaths.
Bolsonaro lost the presidential election to Lula by a narrow margin in the second round.
Bolsonaro supporters stormed the Congress, Supreme Court, and Presidential Palace in an attempt to destabilize the incoming government.

subperiods.name: Third Lula Government
subperiods.description: Lula returned to the presidency for an unprecedented third term

events.description: Brazil rejoined international climate commitments and pledged to end illegal deforestation in the Amazon by 2030.
Brazil assumed the G20 presidency and hosted the summit, using it to promote a global minimum tax on billionaires and debt relief for developing countries.
